metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the `round_a` AC marker in child 19dd with the Approach's natural language ('Round A/B fan-in'); either update the marker to a string that will naturally appear in prose (e.g., `'Round A'`) or instruct the author to embed `round_a` as a code-anchor label in the section.
evidence: 19dd AC: 'markers=["round_a","logical criterion id","effective routing","criteria eval","configuration error"]'
19dd Approach aspect 1: 'Round A/B fan-in + logical id — activated project criteria are fanned into the finder passes…' — uses 'Round A/B', not 'round_a'.
The AC script performs a literal substring match: `if m not in sec` — the string `'round_a'` must appear verbatim in the first 4000 characters of the new section.
No instruction in the Approach or Scope tells the author to embed `round_a` as a literal anchor string in the documentation.
findings.finding: Child 19dd's AC verification script checks for the literal marker string `'round_a'` (lowercase with underscore) within the new documentation section, but the child's own Approach section describes this aspect as 'Round A/B fan-in + logical id' using natural English prose. Documentation text is unlikely to contain the exact substring `round_a` unless the author deliberately embeds it as a code literal, anchor, or label — creating a hidden authoring constraint not surfaced in the Approach.
findings.location: child 19dd, Acceptance Criteria AC1 (verification command marker list)
scenarios: The author writes natural prose ('Round A' or 'Round A/B') and the AC verification script fails at the `round_a` marker even though the section substantively covers the fan-in aspect, causing a false AC failure.
The author notices the marker and embeds `round_a` as an awkward literal in the prose or a code snippet, producing stilted documentation.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the marker from `'round_a'` to `'Round A'` in the AC verification script, or add a note in the Approach that the section must include a code-style label such as `<!-- round_a -->` or a heading containing `round_a` to satisfy the mechanical check.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: etc.
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the RED→GREEN test-slice requirement applies to all six seam children or only the five code-change children (1-5); if 19dd (docs) is exempt, state that exception explicitly in the Decomposition testing-strategy sentence.
evidence: 'each growing one RED→GREEN slice of the shared test file `tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py`' — applies to 'the six seam children'
child 6: 'Docs contract (`docs/llm-framework.md`) — depends on 5. — `19dd`' — no production code; no runtime behaviour to unit-test
findings.finding: The Decomposition section says 'each [of the six seam children] growing one RED→GREEN slice of the shared test file `tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py`', but child 6 (19dd) is a pure docs child (`docs/llm-framework.md`). It is not credible that a docs-only change would grow a slice of a unit test for project-criteria runtime behaviour, so the testing-strategy claim contradicts the nature of child 6.
findings.location: Decomposition section — 'The six seam children land as an ordered Gerrit stack, each single-concern on a disjoint production-file set, each growing one RED→GREEN slice…' vs. seam child 6 description '**Docs contract** (`docs/llm-framework.md`)'
scenarios: Implementer of 19dd writes a spurious test to satisfy the stated constraint, adding noise to the test file.
Implementer skips the test for 19dd, violating the stated constraint and creating inconsistency at plan-review time.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'the six seam children' to 'each code-change seam child (1–5)' in the testing-strategy sentence, and add a parenthetical for child 6: '(child 6, docs-only, is exempt from this requirement)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to fc72's Scope a requirement to update the ids.py module docstring to include the code-review namespace mapping example (`project.foo + code_review → code-review-project-foo`) alongside the existing plan-review example.
evidence: ids.py docstring (lines 13-16): 'built-in F1 → plan-review-F1 (unchanged) / project project.foo → plan-review-project-foo (the one namespace dot → -)' — only plan-review prefix shown.
fc72 Approach adds _PROMPT_PREFIX = {"plan_review": "plan-review-", "code_review": "code-review-"} and a gate_key param, making code-review-project-foo equally valid.
fc72 Scope: 'src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py — add gate_key param + _PROMPT_PREFIX map' — does not mention updating the module docstring examples.

The module docstring is authoritative for both gates' callers; leaving it plan-review-only is a pink-elephant antipattern: the docstring tells the reader the wrong canonical form for code-review calls.
findings.finding: The module docstring of `ids.py` (the file fc72 modifies) documents the logical→physical mapping with explicit examples that only show the plan-review namespace (`project.foo → plan-review-project-foo`). After fc72's changes add the code-review prefix, the docstring examples will document only one of two valid transformations, creating an instruction-locality contradiction for any implementer who reads the docstring to understand the function's behavior.
findings.location: child fc72 — Scope section; src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py:13-16 (docstring examples)
scenarios: A future author reads the ids.py docstring to understand how to call criterion_prompt_id for code review, sees only plan-review-project-foo, and either calls it without gate_key (wrong prefix) or doubts the code-review path is correct.
An eval-admission implementer (8153) consults the docstring to understand what physical id to expect and builds logic matching plan-review-project-foo instead of code-review-project-foo.
findings.suggested_fix: In the docstring's example table, add a third row: 'project project.foo + gate_key="code_review" → code-review-project-foo' so both gates are documented at the authoritative source.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency from child 225a on child 19dd, since both modify `docs/llm-framework.md` and 225a's subsection is logically subordinate to 19dd's section structure.
evidence: 19dd Scope: '`docs/llm-framework.md` — add the "Code-review project criteria" contract section.'
225a Scope: 'Add a `Project dogfood: review-phase-boundaries` subsection to `docs/llm-framework.md`…'
225a Dependency: 'Depends on the linked integration-seam story `2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02`…' — references superseded tickets, not 19dd.
Parent decomposition: '19dd — depends on 5 [41e2]' and '225a — depends on 5 (eval/CLI surface)' — same declared prerequisite, no 225a→19dd edge.

Parent claim: 'No two seam children touch the same production file' — this disjoint claim covers only the seam stack (children 1–6); 225a is feature child 7. The file overlap between 19dd (seam) and 225a (feature) is real.
findings.finding: Child 225a modifies `docs/llm-framework.md` (adds 'Project dogfood: review-phase-boundaries' subsection) and child 19dd also modifies `docs/llm-framework.md` (adds 'Code-review project criteria' section), yet 225a does not declare a dependency on 19dd. Since both children share a declared prerequisite of child 41e2 (seam child 5), they could be applied in either order, risking merge conflicts or a structurally incomplete doc file if 225a lands before 19dd.
scenarios: If 225a is submitted to Gerrit before 19dd merges (both depend only on 41e2), 225a's patch to llm-framework.md will fail to apply cleanly against the pre-19dd version of the file, or if forced, will omit the 'Code-review project criteria' section scaffold that 225a's subsection is meant to sit under.
A reviewer merging both simultaneously in a stack without ordering will produce a structurally inconsistent documentation file.
findings.suggested_fix: In child 225a's Dependency section, add: 'Also depends on child 19dd [rebar:19dd-7d89-972b-4274], which establishes the `Code-review project criteria` section in `docs/llm-framework.md` that this subsection extends.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace references to superseded tickets 2c14 and 8a4b in children 225a and 9447 with explicit dependencies on the seam children that deliver the repair (specifically child 41e2 [rebar:41e2-a0a2-2db4-4847] for the CLI/eval surface, which is the last seam prerequisite both children need).
evidence: 225a Dependency: 'Depends on the linked integration-seam story `2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02`, whose child bug `8a4b-e8b6-1bf9-48d8` owns the concrete repair.'

9447 Dependencies: 'Depends on both the generic integration-seam story `2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02` (including its child bug `8a4b-e8b6-1bf9-48d8`) and the project-criterion authoring story `225a-323c-a9f6-436b`.'
Parent Context: 'This epic is the single umbrella for both, superseding jira-reb-1001 (the prior umbrella), story 2c14 (the generic-seam story), and bug 8a4b (the seam defect).'

Parent Decomposition: 'Six seam children land as an ordered Gerrit stack… The gate-keyed resolver is first because the others consume it; downstream children reference its new symbol in forward/usage tense with a `[rebar:<id>]` citation over a direct `depends_on` edge.'
findings.finding: Children 225a and 9447 reference superseded tickets `2c14-fa5f-1228-4e02` and `8a4b-e8b6-1bf9-48d8` as active live dependencies in their Dependency sections. The parent epic states that 2c14 and 8a4b are superseded by this epic and its seam children, meaning their repair is delivered by fc72/761e/58ef/8153/41e2 — not by the superseded tickets themselves. Referencing closed/superseded tickets as live depends-on edges creates traceability confusion and may cause dependency-resolution tooling to flag a blocked child against a closed ticket.
scenarios: If rebar's dependency resolver checks that depends-on targets are open/active before permitting a child to move to in-progress, children 225a and 9447 may be blocked against the closed 2c14/8a4b tickets.

A developer tracing 225a or 9447's dependency chain via `rebar show` encounters closed/superseded tickets rather than the live seam children that actually deliver the repair, obscuring the real dependency graph.
findings.suggested_fix: In 225a's Dependency section, replace the 2c14/8a4b reference with: 'Depends on seam child 41e2 [rebar:41e2-a0a2-2db4-4847] (CLI admission), the last seam prerequisite.' In 9447's Dependencies section, update similarly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or delegate to a named seam child) that specifies an in-session verification step for the parity guarantee — e.g., 'existing integration tests for overlay-absent repos pass unmodified, verified by `pytest tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py -k overlay_absent` (or equivalent) with zero new skips/failures'.
evidence: 'An overlay-absent repo behaves byte-identically to today; the 13 built-in overlays are unchanged (parity).' — no AC maps to this claim
The four ACs cover: child existence/PASS attestation, file disjointness, supersession closure, and feature-child dependency ordering — none address parity
The decomposition mentions 'growing one RED→GREEN slice of the shared test file' per child but does not call out a regression/parity test slice
findings.finding: The success criterion 'An overlay-absent repo behaves byte-identically to today; the 13 built-in overlays are unchanged (parity)' is a strong behavioral guarantee with no corresponding AC verification step and no delegation to a named child, leaving the evaluating agent unable to determine in-session whether parity holds.
findings.location: Success Criteria, paragraph 2: 'An overlay-absent repo behaves byte-identically to today; the 13 built-in overlays are unchanged (parity).'
scenarios: A reviewer or CI system cannot determine from the epic's ACs alone whether the parity guarantee is tested anywhere
An implementing agent may assume existing tests cover parity without adding any, leaving the guarantee unverifiable at merge time
Post-merge, a regression in overlay-absent behavior would not be caught by any epic-level check
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '[ ] Overlay-absent parity: running the pre-existing overlay-absent test suite (e.g., `pytest tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py -k no_overlay`) against the final seam stack shows zero regressions; no built-in criterion prompt or routing table is modified, verified by `git diff HEAD~6 -- criteria/` showing no changes to the 13 built-in overlay files.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion for the parity guarantee: specify how to verify that an overlay-absent repo is byte-identical to today (e.g., a specific test run or a named regression test in `test_code_review_project_criteria.py`) and that the 13 built-in overlays produce unchanged output.
evidence: 'An overlay-absent repo behaves byte-identically to today; the 13 built-in overlays are unchanged (parity).' — Success Criteria
AC bullets cover: (1) all eight children pass review-plan, (2) root-cause seam points, (3) supersession/closure, (4) feature children ordering — none mention parity or overlay-absent behaviour.
findings.finding: The Success Criteria promise 'An overlay-absent repo behaves byte-identically to today; the 13 built-in overlays are unchanged (parity)' but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this parity guarantee. An implementer has no commanded verification step to confirm backward-compatibility.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'An overlay-absent repo behaves byte-identically to today; the 13 built-in overlays are unchanged (parity).' vs. Acceptance Criteria section (four bullets, none covering parity)
scenarios: A seam change accidentally alters behaviour for repos without an overlay; no AC catches the regression because parity has no verification step.
Reviewer cannot sign off on the epic because the parity promise is unverifiable from the stated ACs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC bullet such as: '- [ ] Overlay-absent parity holds: running `rebar <review command>` on a repo without `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` produces byte-identical output to the pre-epic baseline; verified by the `test_parity_no_overlay` and `test_builtin_overlays_unchanged` test cases in `tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py` passing green.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., a feature flag in `criteria_routing.json` schema, a named revert-commit recipe, or an expand-contract hold period) covering overlay-present repos in the event the seam changes introduce a regression.
evidence: Parity guarantee scoped to 'overlay-absent repo' only
Five named root-cause drop points are all changed in a single Gerrit stack with no described back-out procedure
T4 rubric: 'The remedy for a destructive/breaking change is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing — acknowledgement alone is insufficient'
No mention of feature flag, phased rollout, or revert recipe for overlay-present repos
findings.finding: The plan changes five runtime call sites that affect any repo with a `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` overlay, but provides no rollback/back-out path if the seam changes introduce a regression in production. The parity guarantee covers overlay-absent repos only; for overlay-present repos the behavior changes significantly and there is no described mechanism to revert (e.g., a feature flag, a revert-commit recipe, or an expand-contract hold period).
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'An overlay-absent repo behaves byte-identically to today'; Decomposition — seam stack children fc72/761e/58ef/8153/41e2
scenarios: A project's `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` activates a net-new criterion; after the seam lands, the criterion fires unexpectedly in a gate, causing false-blocking findings; there is no documented way to quickly disable the new path short of reverting the entire stack.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Success Criteria or a new 'Rollback Plan' section, specify: (1) a boolean `enabled` field in the overlay schema (default `false`) so project criteria can be staged off without reverting code, OR (2) a Git revert recipe (revert fc72→41e2 in reverse order) with a note that overlay-absent repos are unaffected, enabling surgical rollback without touching other stack work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or a dedicated ADR section in an existing 0015/0016/0017 update) capturing the decision to introduce the `project.<name>` gate-keyed resolver as the extension seam, including alternatives considered and consequences, so the architectural rationale is preserved alongside the runtime contract in docs/llm-framework.md.
evidence: Plan cites 'ADRs 0015/0016/0017' as establishing the existing code-review extension contract
Child 6 (19dd) is described only as 'Docs contract (`docs/llm-framework.md`)' — a usage doc, not a decision record
No ADR child or ADR update is listed in the decomposition for the gate-key resolver pattern or `project.<name>` namespace decision
The plan introduces the `project.<name>` id namespace and gate-key resolver as a reusable foundation — a novel architectural decision not covered by the three cited ADRs
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new first-class architectural pattern — the gate-keyed `project.<name>` criterion-id namespace as a reusable extension seam — but plans no ADR to capture the decision rationale, alternatives considered, or consequences. Child 19dd updates `docs/llm-framework.md` (a runtime-contract/usage doc) but that is not an architectural decision record. The plan itself cites ADRs 0015/0016/0017 as precedents for the existing system, establishing that this codebase uses ADRs for architectural decisions of this kind.
scenarios: A future engineer adding a second project criterion wonders why the gate-key resolver approach was chosen over a plugin registry or decorator pattern — no ADR exists to answer this
Boundary drift in a future refactor because the rationale for the seam design was never recorded
Reviewers of child fc72 have no ADR to reference when evaluating whether the gate-key approach is the right abstraction
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new child (or expand child 19dd's scope) to author an ADR documenting: (1) the problem — project criteria were silently dropped at five runtime points; (2) the decision — a gate-keyed resolver in `criteria/ids.py` as a single interface boundary; (3) alternatives considered — plugin registry, decorator, separate routing table; (4) consequences — disjoint file ownership, `project.<name>` namespace, parity guarantee. Reference it from the existing ADR 0015/0016/0017 chain.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC line (or name a specific test slice in `test_code_review_project_criteria.py`) that verifies overlay-absent repo behaviour is byte-identical to today — e.g. a parametrised test asserting all 13 built-in routing entries are unchanged and no project-criteria path is entered when no overlay is present.
evidence: 'An overlay-absent repo behaves byte-identically to today; the 13 built-in overlays are unchanged (parity)' — Success Criteria

AC line 1 verifies eight children exist and seam children pass plan-review; AC line 2 verifies disjoint file coverage; AC line 3 verifies supersedes edges; AC line 4 verifies feature-child ordering — none address parity regression

The shared test file `tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py` is mentioned as growing RED→GREEN slices per seam child, but the plan does not state that overlay-absent-path regression coverage is included in those slices
findings.finding: The Success Criteria states 'An overlay-absent repo behaves byte-identically to today; the 13 built-in overlays are unchanged (parity)' as a non-deferred goal, but no AC line specifies a verification step, regression test, or command that demonstrates this parity. The four AC lines cover: seam-child PASS attestations, disjoint file coverage, supersedes edges, and feature-child ordering — none verify overlay-absent runtime parity.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'An overlay-absent repo behaves byte-identically to today' / Acceptance Criteria (all four AC lines)
scenarios: A reviewer or CI system attempting to verify the epic's completion has no command or test result to consult for the parity claim, leaving the success criterion unattestable without an explicit AC gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] Overlay-absent parity: `tests/unit/test_code_review_project_criteria.py` includes at least one parametrised test asserting that, when no `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` overlay is present, the routing table, prompt-id resolution, and Round A/B fan-in are byte-identical to the pre-epic baseline; verified by the shared test file's green run in the absence of any project overlay fixture.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-to-two sentence positive rationale in the Decomposition section explaining *why* the ordered Gerrit stack with disjoint production files and a single growing test file is the right structural approach for this epic (e.g., green-at-every-step guarantee, independent reviewability, reduced blast radius per merge).
evidence: The plan states the structure: 'ordered Gerrit stack, each single-concern on a disjoint production-file set, each growing one RED→GREEN slice of the shared test file' — but gives no affirmative benefit claim for this pattern vs. alternatives.
Child fc72's Approach section does explain the disjoint-file rationale for its specific scope choice, but the epic-level rationale for the stacked-children strategy itself is absent.
ADR 0015/0016/0017 and the referenced registry story 5065 are cited as context but not as justification for the decomposition strategy.
findings.finding: The epic plan's Decomposition section describes the ordered Gerrit stack structure (what it is) but does not state a positive rationale for why this decomposition strategy — six disjoint single-concern children in a Gerrit stack with a shared growing test file — is the right approach for this epic, as opposed to a monolithic seam-fix PR or a feature-flag rollout. Without that rationale, an implementer or reviewer cannot evaluate whether the structural approach is fit for purpose on its own terms.
findings.location: Decomposition section, paragraph beginning 'Eight children. The six seam children land as an ordered Gerrit stack'
scenarios: A reviewer or approver of the epic plan cannot verify that the stack-and-disjoint-files approach is the best structural choice without an explicit claim about why it is better than a single combined PR (e.g., reduced review surface, green-at-every-step guarantees, smaller blast radius per merge).
If the Gerrit stack ordering changes or a child needs to be promoted, the absence of a documented rationale makes it harder to evaluate what can change without breaking the approach's key property.
findings.suggested_fix: After the sentence 'The gate-keyed resolver is first because the others consume it', add: 'This stack strategy is chosen because it guarantees the integration suite is green after every individual merge (each child adds exactly one RED→GREEN test slice), enables independent code-review of each seam point, and bounds the blast radius of any single change to one production-file set — properties a monolithic five-file PR would lose.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in child 8153 how `calibrate_criterion` determines a criterion_id belongs to the code-review gate before calling `criterion_prompt_id` with `gate_key='code_review'` — e.g., by consulting `code_review.registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)` (consistent with 41e2's dual-registry lookup) or by accepting an explicit gate parameter.
evidence: 8153 Approach: 'add a code-review branch that resolves the physical prompt id via criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review") — when the id belongs to the code-review gate'

eval.py:416-420 (current code): branches on criterion_id.startswith('plan-review-') — a physical-id prefix test. A project id like 'project.foo' has no such prefix, and whether it's plan-review or code-review cannot be determined by inspection of the id alone.
effective_criteria() for each gate returns overlapping namespaces (both gates can activate 'project.foo') — the ambiguous CLI case in 41e2 proves this overlap is real.
No lookup protocol is specified in 8153 (e.g., 'check code_review.registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)' first, or 'accept a gate parameter').
The current calibrate_criterion signature has no gate parameter, and 8153's scope does not specify adding one.
findings.finding: The `calibrate_criterion` branching decision rule in `eval.py` (child 8153) is not specified: the plan says 'add a code-review branch that resolves via criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, gate_key="code_review") when the id belongs to the code-review gate', but never defines how the function determines that an incoming criterion_id belongs to the code-review gate vs. the plan-review gate, before the gate-keyed resolver is called.
scenarios: A project criterion id 'project.bar' is passed to calibrate_criterion; the function cannot determine whether to call criterion_prompt_id with gate_key='plan_review' or gate_key='code_review' without a specified lookup protocol.

Implementer must invent the branching rule (e.g., look it up in both registries, or accept a gate param) with no plan guidance — risking a decision that diverges from 41e2's 'active in exactly one gate' CLI logic.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to 8153's Approach: 'Determine gate membership by checking `code_review.registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)` first; if the id is present there, use gate_key="code_review". This mirrors the 41e2 dual-registry lookup and ensures the two surfaces agree on gate assignment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in child 8153's Approach that `_code_review_prompt_id` must translate each logical id from `effective_criteria()` to its physical prompt id via `criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key='code_review')` before comparing against the incoming `prompt_id`, so the extension is not a silent no-op.
evidence: eval_solver.py:187-201: `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id: str)` — takes an already-physical prompt id (e.g. 'code-review-foo').
code_review.registry.effective_criteria() returns logical ids: ('scope-intent', 'security', ..., 'project.foo') — the project.<name> logical form.
8153 Approach: 'extend `_code_review_prompt_id` to also admit ids present in the code-review effective registry — consult registry.effective_criteria(repo_root)' — no translation specified.

A logical id 'project.foo' != physical id 'code-review-project-foo'; comparing them directly will always fail, making the extension a no-op unless criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key='code_review') is called on each logical id to build a comparison set.
findings.finding: The `_code_review_prompt_id` function in `eval_solver.py` receives a `prompt_id` argument (physical id, already prefixed), but `effective_criteria(repo_root)` returns logical ids (`project.foo`). Child 8153 says 'extend `_code_review_prompt_id` to also admit ids present in the code-review effective registry', but never specifies the translation step (logical → physical) needed to compare the incoming physical `prompt_id` against the logical ids returned by the registry.
scenarios: Implementer calls effective_criteria() and checks if prompt_id is in the returned tuple — always False because logical ids differ from physical ids, so project criteria are still rejected.
The extension silently does nothing, making the test in 8153's AC pass only if the test also misunderstands the format.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 8153's Approach: 'Build the admissible physical-id set by calling `criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate_key="code_review") [rebar:fc72]` for each id in `effective_criteria(repo_root)`, then admit `prompt_id` if it is in that set.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign ownership of creating `supersedes` edges from the epic to jira-reb-1001, 2c14, and 8a4b, and closing each — either as an explicit epic-level action item or a new child.
evidence: Parent AC-C: 'The epic supersedes jira-reb-1001, 2c14, and 8a4b, and all three are closed; verified by `rebar show 119f-63e3-34b7-4f96` listing three `supersedes` edges and `rebar show <each>` reporting `status=closed`.'
No child scope section mentions creating supersedes edges or closing these tickets.
Parent Success Criteria SC5: 'jira-reb-1001, 2c14, and 8a4b are superseded by this epic and closed.' — unassigned.
findings.finding: No child in the roster owns or verifies the administrative closure of superseded tickets jira-reb-1001, 2c14, and 8a4b. The parent AC-C requires `rebar show` to confirm three `supersedes` edges and `status=closed` on each, but this action is undelegated — no seam or feature child creates these edges or closes these tickets.
findings.location: Parent Acceptance Criteria AC-C; no child in the roster
scenarios: At epic completion review, AC-C cannot be verified because no child performed the closure actions or established the supersedes edges, leaving the parent's completion verifier unable to confirm the criterion.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an epic-level action (or extend an existing child such as 19dd) to record the three `supersedes` edges via `rebar link --supersedes` and close jira-reb-1001, 2c14, and 8a4b upon seam-stack completion.

findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'dogfooded in advisory Gerrit review (children 225a, 9447)' is not tagged `[operator-attested]` but describes an external, post-session action (an actual advisory Gerrit review must occur), making it non-verifiable in-session via repo artifacts or CI.
findings.location: Success Criteria — third bullet ('The `project.review-phase-boundaries` criterion and its balanced eval corpus exist and are dogfooded in advisory Gerrit review')
findings.suggested_fix: Prefix the bullet: '[operator-attested] The `project.review-phase-boundaries` criterion and its balanced eval corpus exist and are dogfooded in advisory Gerrit review; attested by Gerrit change-id recorded on child 9447.'

findings.finding: The Success Criteria describe specific behavioral outcomes of the seam fix — project criterion executes once through Round A/B, carries its `project.<name>` id on every finding, has its threshold/blocking/nit-suppression govern Pass 3, resolves through the eval/calibration path, and is admitted by the CLI — but none of the three Acceptance Criteria verify any of these runtime behaviors. The ACs only verify that children exist, pass plan-review, cover the five drop points, and close superseded items. The core behavioral promise has no covering AC.
findings.location: Success Criteria (first bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all three items)

findings.finding: No child owns the work of creating `supersedes` edges or setting status=closed on jira-reb-1001, story 2c14, and bug 8a4b. The parent's acceptance criterion AC-C ('The epic supersedes jira-reb-1001, 2c14, and 8a4b, and all three are closed; verified by `rebar show 119f...` listing three `supersedes` edges and `rebar show <each>` reporting status=closed') requires a concrete action that no child's scope or AC covers.
findings.location: Parent AC-C; no child in the sibling roster
findings.suggested_fix: Either assign the closure/supersession action to child 19dd (the docs/process child at the end of the seam stack) or create a ninth 'close-out' child that explicitly performs `rebar supersede` and `rebar close` for all three tickets and verifies the resulting state.

findings.finding: The Success Criteria guarantees 'An overlay-absent repo behaves byte-identically to today; the 13 built-in overlays are unchanged (parity)' but no Acceptance Criterion verifies this parity guarantee. This is a described deliverable (regression safety) with no covering criterion.
findings.location: Success Criteria (second bullet) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all three items)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
